description:
'''{{tlit|ar|Fijiri}}''' ({{langx|ar|الفجيري|rtl=yes}}; sometimes spelled '''{{tlit|ar|fijri}}''', '''{{tlit|ar|fidjeri}}''', or '''{{tlit|ar|fjiri}}''') is the specific repertoire of [[vocal music]] sung by the [[pearl divers]] of Eastern Arabia's [[Arab states of the Persian Gulf|coastal Gulf states]], especially [[Bahrain]] and [[Kuwait]]. A lead singer is backed up by a [[choir|chorus]] of accompanying singers and clapping. The accompanying instruments to a {{tlit|ar|fidjeri}} ensemble are a small double-sided hand-[[drum]], known as the {{tlit|ar|[[mirwas|mirwās]]}} ({{lang|ar|المرواس|rtl=yes}}) and the {{tlit|ar|jāhlah}} ({{lang|ar|الجاهلة|rtl=yes}}), a clay pot played with both hands.

There are eight genres of {{tlit|ar|fijiri}}: {{tlit|ar|sanginni}} (sung on the beach, not on the boat), {{tlit|ar|bahri}}, {{tlit|ar|adsani}}, {{tlit|ar|mkholfi}}, {{tlit|ar|haddadi}}, {{tlit|ar|hasawi}}, {{tlit|ar|zumayya}}, and {{tlit|ar|dan}}; the last two are viewed as subgenres of {{tlit|ar|hasawi}} and {{tlit|ar|mkholfi}} respectively. {{tlit|ar|Bahri}} and {{tlit|ar|adsani}} are the two main genres.{{cn|date=May 2026}} Pearl diver singers are referred to in Arabic as {{tlit|ar|nahham}} ({{lang|ar|نهام|rtl=yes}}). [[Salem Allan]] and Ahmad Butabbaniya are two of the most well-known {{tlit|ar|fijiri}} singers from Bahrain.
